fix: Persist pipeline_status cleanup when deleting a dataset (#2230)

## Summary

- When a dataset is deleted, `delete_dataset()` clears pipeline_status
entries for the deleted dataset from related Data objects. However, the
nested dict deletion (`del status[pipeline_name][dataset_id]`) was not
detected by SQLAlchemy's `MutableDict` change tracking, which only
monitors top-level dict operations. The cleanup ran in memory but was
never persisted to the database on commit.
- Since dataset IDs are deterministic (`uuid5` of name + user_id +
tenant_id), recreating a dataset with the same name produces the **same
ID**. Without persisted cleanup, re-added data was incorrectly skipped
by the incremental loading cache, which still saw the old "completed"
pipeline_status entry.
- Fix: use `flag_modified(data_record, "pipeline_status")` from
`sqlalchemy.orm.attributes` to explicitly mark the column as dirty after
nested dict modifications.

## Root Cause

`Data.pipeline_status` is a `MutableDict.as_mutable(JSON)` column.
`MutableDict` tracks top-level dict operations (`__setitem__`,
`__delitem__`, `setdefault`, etc.) but **not** modifications to nested
dicts. The deletion pattern:

```python
del data_record.pipeline_status[pipeline_name][dataset_id_str]
#    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^                               → MutableDict.__getitem__ (read, no change event)
#                                 ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^    → plain dict.__delitem__ (no change event)
```

produces no change notification, so `session.commit()` sees no dirty
columns and skips the UPDATE.

## Test plan

- [ ] Add data to a named dataset, cognify it, delete the dataset,
re-add the same data to a dataset with the same name — verify the data
is processed again (not skipped as cached)
- [ ] Verify Data.pipeline_status no longer contains the deleted
dataset's ID after deletion
- [ ] Run existing unit/integration tests

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

-->

no other dataset references remain.

persisted so pipeline/status updates are saved.
* Batch deletion now correctly propagates dataset context to per-item
deletion, preventing orphaned or partially-deleted records.

* **Tests**
* Updated tests to validate dataset-scoped deletion behavior and file
removal across datasets.

<!-- .github/pull_request_template.md -->

## Description
This PR Implements automatic feedback generation for Q&A sessions.

Adds a FeedbackDetectionResult model and a new feedback_detection module
that uses an LLM to analyze user messages and detect explicit feedback
(e.g., ratings or corrections). When enabled via
CacheConfig.auto_feedback, the session manager calls the detection
pipeline after generating a reply, computes a 1–5 score, summarizes the
feedback, and stores it along with the last Q&A ID.


## Acceptance Criteria
- If auto_feedback and caching are enabled, user messages that clearly
evaluate the previous answer are detected and stored with a numeric
score and summary.
- Follow‑up questions or unrelated messages do not trigger feedback
storage.
- A friendly response_to_user is generated only when feedback is
detected.
- Setting auto_feedback=False disables feedback detection.
- All new and old tests pass.

commit.message: Update test_cache_config.py
Update config.py
chore: sets default auto feedback setting to false

fix: Persist pipeline_status cleanup when deleting a dataset

The delete_dataset() function clears pipeline_status entries for the
deleted dataset from related Data objects. However, the nested dict
deletion (del status[pipeline_name][dataset_id]) was not detected by
SQLAlchemy's MutableDict change tracking, which only monitors top-level
dict operations. This meant the cleanup was performed in memory but
never persisted to the database on commit.

Since dataset IDs are deterministic (uuid5 of name + user_id +
tenant_id), recreating a dataset with the same name produces the same
ID. Without persisted cleanup, re-added data was incorrectly skipped
by the incremental loading cache which still saw the old "completed"
pipeline_status entry.

Fix: use flag_modified() to explicitly mark the pipeline_status column
as dirty after nested dict modifications.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Signed-off-by: vasilije <vas.markovic@gmail.com>

releases.body:
# Release Notes - v0.5.4.dev1

**Release Date:** 2026-03-05
**Changes:** v0.5.4.dev1 → dev

---

## Summary

This is a developer preview release (v0.5.4.dev1) focused on iterative quality, performance, and developer ergonomics. It contains targeted feature additions for import and search, several reliability fixes, and internal refactors to make future releases faster and safer to ship.

## Highlights

- Developer preview release with targeted improvements and fixes
- Faster ingestion and sync, reducing delay when adding memories
- Improved search relevance and new filtering options for more accurate results
- Stability and bug fixes for memory creation, deletion, and CLI workflows
- Internal refactoring and dependency upgrades that improve maintainability and prepare for upcoming features

## Breaking Changes

- No breaking changes in this dev preview. Existing integrations and APIs should continue to work as before.

## New Features

- Bulk import CLI: a new import mode for faster, batched ingestion of large memory sets (CLI-focused developer tool).
- Search filters: added basic tag and date-range filtering to search queries to help narrow results.
- Per-collection ingestion throttling: optional rate limits to smooth ingestion spikes and reduce timeouts during heavy load.

## Improvements

- Ingestion and sync performance: reduced latency when saving and syncing memories across the system, so newly added items appear faster.
- Search relevance: tweaks to ranking heuristics for more relevant results on common queries.
- Stability: more robust handling around memory deletion and duplicate detection to avoid orphaned data.
- Developer experience: clearer CLI messages, improved debug logs, and small SDK ergonomics improvements to speed up development and troubleshooting.

## Bug Fixes

- Fixed an issue that could create duplicate memories under certain concurrent ingestion patterns.
- Resolved cases where memory deletion left partial state, causing inconsistent search results.
- Fixed CLI export formatting bugs that occasionally corrupted exported files.
- Addressed intermittent memory retrieval failures that surfaced under high load.

## Technical Changes

- Indexing pipeline refactor: internal reorganization of the memory indexing code to improve performance and make future features easier to add.
- Dependency upgrades: several internal libraries have been updated for security and performance—please run your usual test suite against the dev build.
- Expanded automated tests around ingestion and search to reduce regressions going forward.
- Improved logging: unified log format and additional diagnostic information to help troubleshooting.

---

*Generated by Cognee Release Notes Generator on 2026-03-05*

releases.name: v0.5.3.dev0
releases.body:
# 🚀 Release 0.5.3 (Development)

## 🌟 Highlights
This release introduces a robust **SessionManager** for enhanced data persistence, significant upgrades to **Graph Visualization** (including node coloring), and a major refactor of user/tenant permission systems.

## 🚀 New Features
- **Session Management**: Implemented `SessionManager` with CRUD operations for `CacheAdapters` and Redis integration ([#2077](https://github.com), [#2082](https://github.com), [#2142](https://github.com)).
- **Graph & Visualization**: 
  - New graph visualization frontend using `three.js` ([#1671](https://github.com), [#1680](https://github.com)).
  - Added node set coloring for better visual data distinction ([#2129](https://github.com)).
  - Support for batch queries in all graph completion retrievers ([#2002](https://github.com)).
- **User & Tenant Management**: 
  - Added role-based permission checks and endpoints to get roles for specific `tenant_id` ([#2078](https://github.com), [#2132](https://github.com)).
  - New endpoint to remove users from tenants ([#2163](https://github.com)).
- **API Enhancements**: 
  - Added return type hints to API endpoint functions for better developer experience ([#2072](https://github.com)).
  - New flag for automatic dataset deletion when left empty ([#2167](https://github.com)).

## 🛠 Bug Fixes
- **Stability**: Prevented indefinite hangs with local LLM endpoints ([#2123](https://github.com)).
- **Data Integrity**: Resolved multiple bugs in the delete data and dataset flows ([#2146](https://github.com)).
- **Infrastructure**: Fixed configuration bugs in `config.py` and missing raise statements in vector DB setup ([#2053](https://github.com), [#2080](https://github.com)).
- **Adapters**: Fixed a `TypeError` in `NeptuneAnalyticsAdapter.is_empty()` ([#2172](https://github.com)).
- **Security**: Addressed various Dependabot security vulnerabilities ([#2124](https://github.com)).

## 📑 Documentation
- Added comprehensive READMEs for `summarization`, `web_scraper`, and `codingagents` tasks ([#2137](https://github.com), [#2184](https://github.com)).
- Added module-level docstrings for ingestion, storage, and temporal tasks ([#2086](https://github.com), [#2177](https://github.com)).

## 🧹 Refactors & Chore
- **Cleanup**: Removed old feedback code and graph-based interaction logic ([#2089](https://github.com)).
- **CI/CD**: Upgraded GitHub Actions for Node 24 compatibility and improved CI stability ([#2108](https://github.com), [#2145](https://github.com)).

readme_html:
Cognee - Build AI memory with a Knowledge Engine that learns

Use our knowledge engine to build personalized and dynamic memory for AI Agents.

About Cognee

Cognee is an open-source knowledge engine that lets you ingest data in any format or structure and continuously learns to provide the right context for AI agents. It combines vector search, graph databases and cognitive science approaches to make your documents both searchable by meaning and connected by relationships as they change and evolve.

:star: Help us reach more developers and grow the cognee community. Star this repo!

Why use Cognee:

  • Knowledge infrastructure — unified ingestion, graph/vector search, runs locally, ontology grounding, multimodal
  • Persistent and Learning Agents - learn from feedback, context management, cross-agent knowledge sharing
  • Reliable and Trustworthy Agents - agentic user/tenant isolation, traceability, OTEL collector, audit traits

Basic Usage & Feature Guide

To learn more, check out this short, end-to-end Colab walkthrough of Cognee's core features.

Quickstart

Let’s try Cognee in just a few lines of code. For detailed setup and configuration, see the Cognee Docs.

Prerequisites

  • Python 3.10 to 3.13

Step 1: Install Cognee

You can install Cognee with pip, poetry, uv, or your preferred Python package manager.

uv pip install cognee

Step 2: Configure the LLM

import os
os.environ["LLM_API_KEY"] = "YOUR OPENAI_API_KEY"

Alternatively, create a .env file using our template.

To integrate other LLM providers, see our LLM Provider Documentation.

Step 3: Run the Pipeline

Cognee will take your documents, load them into the knowledge angine and search combined vector/graph relationships.

Now, run a minimal pipeline:

import cognee
import asyncio
from pprint import pprint


async def main():
    # Add text to cognee
    await cognee.add("Cognee turns documents into AI memory.")

    # Add to knowledge engine
    await cognee.cognify()

    # Query the knowledge graph
    results = await cognee.search("What does Cognee do?")

    # Display the results
    for result in results:
        pprint(result)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    asyncio.run(main())

As you can see, the output is generated from the document we previously stored in Cognee:

  Cognee turns documents into AI memory.

Use the Cognee CLI

As an alternative, you can get started with these essential commands:

cognee-cli add "Cognee turns documents into AI memory."

cognee-cli cognify

cognee-cli search "What does Cognee do?"
cognee-cli delete --all

To open the local UI, run:

cognee-cli -ui
